name: Plucking corn on the sabbath
notes: A threefold synoptic retelling: as Jesus goes through the corn fields on the sabbath, his hungry disciples pluck and eat the ears of corn, the Pharisees object that this is unlawful, and the Lord answers with David eating the shewbread and with his own lordship over the sabbath. All three accounts share this core, and each keeps complementary detail: Luke notes the disciples were rubbing the ears in their hands, Mark alone records the saying 'The sabbath was made for man, and not man for the sabbath' (here left appropriately unlinked), and Matthew alone adds the priests who profane the sabbath and are blameless and the word 'I will have mercy, and not sacrifice' (12:5-7), which has no parallel and reads in place. Mark and Luke close with 'the Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath' while Matthew expands it to 'Lord even of the sabbath day.' These are complementary vantages on one event, each Evangelist preserving what the Spirit moved him to record.
